title_en: Tahara and Salah — Purity and Prayer
content_en:
The chapters on tahara and salah in Rawdat al-Talibin present the Shafi'i positions on purity and prayer with the precision and comprehensiveness for which Imam al-Nawawi is renowned. The treatment reflects the Shafi'i school's distinctive emphasis on textual grounding and its careful hierarchy of valid proofs.

On tahara, the Shafi'i positions presented in the Rawdah include several that distinguish the school from other madhabs. The Shafi'is hold that the basmalah — saying 'Bismillah al-Rahman al-Rahim' — is the first verse of al-Fatiha and of every surah except at-Tawbah, making its recitation obligatory in prayer. The Shafi'i school also holds that musta'mal water (water used for obligatory purification) becomes a lesser grade of pure water and may no longer be used for obligatory purification, though it remains pure itself — a position differing from the Maliki school's more lenient treatment. Al-Nawawi works through the conditions for the validity of wudu and ghusl with characteristic thoroughness, distinguishing obligatory elements from recommended ones and citing the relevant hadith at each point.

The salah chapters in Rawdat al-Talibin cover the conditions, pillars, and obligations of prayer with the level of detail required by a reference work for trained jurists. The Shafi'i school's position that niyyah (intention) must be made at the opening takbir — not before it — is explained with the underlying reasoning. The Shafi'i requirement to recite al-Fatiha in every rak'ah, including those behind the imam, is presented with the hadith evidence: 'There is no prayer for one who does not recite al-Fatiha' (Bukhari and Muslim). Al-Nawawi also addresses the rules of the latecomer (masbuq), the rules for prayer during travel, and the combining and shortening of prayers, presenting the Shafi'i position on when and how these dispensations apply. The depth and precision of al-Nawawi's treatment of these foundational chapters reflects his conviction that correct religious practice begins with correct understanding of the prophetic model, and that jurists have an obligation to convey that understanding as precisely as the sources allow. Students who work through these chapters carefully gain not only knowledge of Shafi'i rulings but insight into how the school's characteristic emphasis on textual proof operates in practice at the level of individual legal questions.
